coredata.dc:title: N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles co-modified ultrathin Bi2MoO6 nanosheet with enhanced charge transport dynamics for full-spectrum-light-driven molecular oxygen activation
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Bisheng
Lai, Cui
Zhang, Mingming
Liu, Shiyu
Yi, Huan
Liu, Xigui
An, Ning
Zhou, Xuerong
Li, Ling
Fu, Yukui
Qin, Lei
Chen, Liang
coredata.dc:description: It is a great challenge to achieve ultrafast charge transfer and simultaneously strengthen light adsorption for full-spectrum-light-activated photocatalysis. In this work, N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) and Au nanoparticles are successfully co-modified on atomic ultrathin Bi2MoO6 nanosheet, and the prepared hybrid material displays exceptional performance in charge carrier transfer and light harvest. The obtained hybrid material possesses excellent photocatalytic activity in molecular oxygen activation for oxytetracycline (OTC) degradation with 3.69-fold higher reaction rate than pristine Bi2MoO6 under visible light irradiation. In addition, it also possesses near-infrared-light-driven catalytic activity, about 24.47% of OTC can be degraded under near-infrared light irradiation. Moreover, the hybrid material displays outstanding catalytic performance for actual wastewater treatment, and it also possesses high photostability. The mechanism exploration reveals that the boosted photocatalytic performance is originated from three aspects. The up-conversion photoluminescence feature of N, S-GQDs broadens photoresponse range, and the light nanoantenna and SPR effect of Au nanoparticle enhance light absorption intensity. Meanwhile, both N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles accelerate charge carrier transfer, the surface charge transfer efficiency of pristine Bi2MoO6 and hybrid material are calculated to be 33.74% and 53.04%, respectively. Furthermore, the possible photodegradation pathway of OTC is also inquired according to liquid chromatography-triple quadrupole tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). This work provides inspiration to rationally design high-performance full-spectrum-light-driven catalysts in molecular oxygen activation for environmental modification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Charge carrier transfer
Light harvest
Up-conversion photoluminescence property
Light nanoantenna
Full-spectrum-light-activated catalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N, S-GQDs modified ultrathin BMO nanosheet
Preparation of N, S-GQDs and Au co-modified ultrathin BMO nanosheet

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-d) The TEM image of BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3, (e) the HETEM image of BGDA-3 (f-g) the AFM image and the corresponding height profile, (h-o) the EDS element mapping image.
The high resolution XPS spectra of (a) Bi 4f, (b) Mo 3d, (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) S 2p, and (f) Au 4f of the prepared photocatalysts.

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra of pristine BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3, (b) the band gaps of pristine BMO, (c) the Mott-Schottky curves of pristine BMO at different frequencies (500 and 1000 Hz), (d) the XPS-VB spectra of pristine BMO.

(a) The photocatalytic performance of BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3 by degrading OTC under visible light irradiation, (b) and the corresponding -ln(C/C0) versus time curves, (c) the photocatalytic performance of BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3 by degrading OTC under NIR light irradiation, (d) and the corresponding -ln(C/C0) versus time curves.

(a) The photoctalytic performance of BGDA-3 for five wastewaters treatment of tap water (TW), lake water (LW), river water (RW), industrial wastewater (IW), and medical wastewater (MW); (b) the five continuous cycle experiments of BGDA-3 for OTC degradation under the same condition.
(a-b) Effect of different scavengers on the photodegradation efficiency of BGDA-3 for OTC, (c) ESR spectra for DMPO-•O2 – and DMPO-HO•.

Absorption spectra of NBT solution over (a) BMO and (b) BGDA-3 under visible light irradiation; (c) time-dependent degradation curves of NBT by BMO and BGDA-3; and (d) the corresponding •O2 – production.
The diagram of preparation of BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3.
The possible degradation pathways of OTC by BGDA-3 under visible light irradiation.
The possible degradation pathways of OTC by BGDA-3 under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles co-modified ultrathin Bi2MoO6 nanosheet with enhanced charge transport dynamics for full-spectrum-light-driven molecular oxygen activation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University and Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control (Hunan University), Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, Hunan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering
Hunan University and Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control (Hunan University), Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University and Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control (Hunan University), Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, Hunan, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha, Hunan 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha, Hunan 410004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is a great challenge to achieve ultrafast charge transfer and simultaneously strengthen light adsorption for full-spectrum-light-activated photocatalysis. In this work, N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) and Au nanoparticles are successfully co-modified on atomic ultrathin Bi2MoO6 nanosheet, and the prepared hybrid material displays exceptional performance in charge carrier transfer and light harvest. The obtained hybrid material possesses excellent photocatalytic activity in molecular oxygen activation for oxytetracycline (OTC) degradation with 3.69-fold higher reaction rate than pristine Bi2MoO6 under visible light irradiation. In addition, it also possesses near-infrared-light-driven catalytic activity, about 24.47% of OTC can be degraded under near-infrared light irradiation. Moreover, the hybrid material displays outstanding catalytic performance for actual wastewater treatment, and it also possesses high photostability. The mechanism exploration reveals that the boosted photocatalytic performance is originated from three aspects. The up-conversion photoluminescence feature of N, S-GQDs broadens photoresponse range, and the light nanoantenna and SPR effect of Au nanoparticle enhance light absorption intensity. Meanwhile, both N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles accelerate charge carrier transfer, the surface charge transfer efficiency of pristine Bi2MoO6 and hybrid material are calculated to be 33.74% and 53.04%, respectively. Furthermore, the possible photodegradation pathway of OTC is also inquired according to liquid chromatography-triple quadrupole tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). This work provides inspiration to rationally design high-performance full-spectrum-light-driven catalysts in molecular oxygen activation for environmental modification.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid expansion of urbanization and industrialization has caused serious environmental deterioration, which has posed great threaten to the sustainable development of modern society [1,2]. Molecular oxygen has been deemed as one of the greenest and cheapest oxidants. However, oxygen molecule is usually unable to degrade pollutants as a result of its spin forbidden reactions [3,4]. Fortunately, molecular oxygen can be activated to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are ordinarily considered to be highly active oxidizing agents in environmental chemistry and biochemistry [5–7]. Sunlight-driven molecular oxygen activation over photocatalysts has long been considered as an ideal “green strategy” [8,9]. The high-performance and low-cost photocatalyst is critical component in molecular oxygen activation process. As one of Aurivillius member, Bi2MoO6 (BMO) with the layered structure, narrow bad gap, and suitable valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) has become an excellent photocatalyst [10]. Besides, our group also confirmed that ultrathin BMO nanosheet with monolayer or few-layered thickness held promoted catalytic activity because of the shortened charge migration distance from interior to catalyst surface [11]. Nevertheless, the charge carrier will still recombine on catalyst surface, and the light absorption range is also weakened because of the quantum confinement effect [12]. Accordingly, it is essential to further accelerate charge carrier transfer, broaden light absorption range, and strengthen light absorption intensity.

Considering the distinctive two-dimensional (2D) structure of ultrathin BMO nanosheet, the 0D/2D heterojunction construction is believed as an effective strategy to inhibit charge carrier recombination and simultaneously provide more active site for catalytic reaction [13]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with dimensions smaller than 10 nm have attracted considerable attention because of its distinctive features, including outstanding electron transfer and reservoir property, unique up-conversion fluorescence ability, robust chemical inertness, and low-toxicity [14]. Especially, it has been demonstrated that N and S co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs) show superior optical properties and functions in comparison with undoped GQDs, which can be excited by long-wavelength light and emit short-wavelength light [15]. Consequently, combining N, S-GQDs with ultrathin BMO nanosheet to construct 0D/2D heterojunction is a better choice. The excellent electron transfer and reservoir property of N, S-GQDs promotes charge carrier separation and transfer through the electron tunneling mechanism, the unique up-conversion fluorescence capacity broadens light absorption range [16]. Nevertheless, the loading amount of N, S-GQDs on ultrathin BMO nanosheet could be lower than 1% according to previous studies [17]. Therefore, the introduction of N, S-GQDs only broadens light absorption range but does not enhance light absorption intensity.

Plasmonic structure has shown great potential to maximumly utilize sunlight owing to its outstanding light-harvesting and electromagnetic-field-concentrating property originated from surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect [18]. In plasmonic nanostructures, noble metals such as Au function as light nanoantenna and signal amplifier, they first harvest the incident light and then enhance light intensity owing to SPR effect, the enhanced light is absorbed by neighboring semiconductor [19]. More importantly, the association of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles also promotes near-infrared light (NIR) utilization. It’s because that N, S-GQDs can harvest and convert long-wavelength NIR light into short-wavelength ultraviolet (UV) or visible light, and then Au nanoparticles enhance the light intensity, and finally absorbed by semiconductor. The loading of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles on ultrathin BMO nanosheet will play a triple roles: (1) the excellent electron transfer and reservoir feature of N, S-GQDs and the electron sink of Au nanoparticles accelerate charge carrier transfer [20]; (2) the unique up-conversion fluorescence ability of N, S-GQDs broadens light absorption range [21]; (3) Au nanoparticles function as light nanoantenna and signal amplifier to enhance light absorption intensity [22]. These superiorities provide a solid guarantee for excellent photocatalytic performance.

In this work, N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles co-modified ultrathin Bi2MoO6 nanosheet is successfully prepared by hydrothermal method coupling with (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane coupling method. The catalytic activities of the prepared materials are estimated through molecular oxygen activation for OTC degradation. The light-harvesting ability is assessed by the diffuse reflectance spectrum and the charge carrier separation and transfer efficiency is measured by photo-electrochemical measurement. Ultimately, the possible mechanism for the boosted catalytic activity in full-spectrum range is put forward.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), sodiummolybdat dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) are employed as precursors to prepare ultrathin BMO nanosheet. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) and trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O) are used to prepare Au nanoparticles. Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), thiourea (CH4N2S), and N, N-Dimethylformamide (HCON(CH3)2) are utilized to synthesize N, S-GQDs.

The Au nanoparticles were prepared through classic sodium citrate reduction method [23]. 97 mL of ultrapure water and 3 mL of HAuCl4·4H2O (10 g L-1) were added into 250 mL three-necked flask and heated on electronic universal furnace until the solution boiled. Then 9 mL of freshly prepared trisodium citrate solutions was quickly poured into boiling solution. When the solution changed from primrose yellow to wine red, it still heated for another 18 min and then naturally cooled to ambient temperature. The ultimate Au solution was poured into 100 mL brown volumetric flask and stored at 3 ℃ for further use. The concentration of Au solution was determined to be 145 mg L-1 by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS).

The N, S-GQDs was synthetized through solvothermal route [15]. 0.1921 g of citric acid and 0.2284 g of thiourea were added into 4 mL of N, N-Dimethylformamide and stirred for 30 min to make citric acid and thiourea completely dissolve and generate a transparent solution. Then this solution was poured into a 25 mL autoclave. The autoclave was reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. The ethanol was added into the ultimate suspension and then centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 0.5 h. The precipitate was dispersed into ultrapure water and stored at 3 ℃ for further use. The concentration of N, S-GQDs solution was determined to be 2.26 mg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, S-GQDs modified ultrathin BMO nanosheet
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S-GQDs modified ultrathin BMO nanosheet was prepared through CTAB-assisted hydrothermal way [11]. 0.9701 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 0.2420 g of Na2MoO4·2H2O, 0.05 g of CTAB, and different volumes of prepared N, S-GQDs (0.5, 1, 2, 4, and 8 mL) were added into 80 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 1 h. The obtained mixture was added into 100 mL autoclave and reacted at 120 ℃ for 24 h. When it cooled down to ambient temperature, the products were acquired by filtrating and alternately washing with ultrapure water and ethanol until CTAB and impurity were completely eliminated. Finally, the resultant solid was dried at 60 ℃ overnight. The N, S-GQDs modified ultrathin BMO nanosheet with 0.5, 1, 2, 4, and 8 mL of N, S-GQDs were nominated as BGD-0.5, BGD-1, BGD-2, BGD-4, and BGD-8, respectively. Meanwhile, the pristine ultrathin BMO nanosheet was prepared using the similar process but without adding N, S-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, S-GQDs and Au co-modified ultrathin BMO nanosheet
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S-GQDs and Au co-modified ultrathin BMO nanosheet was prepared via (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane coupling method (Scheme 1 ) [24]. 0.2 g of prepared BGD-1 was uniformly dispersed into 80 mL of ultrapure water through ultrasonic sound, and then 1 mL of (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane was added into above-mentioned mixture. The suspension was first stirred at room temperature for 2 h and then stirred at 60 ℃ for another 2 h. The modified BGD-1 was washed for several times and re-dispersed into 80 mL of ultrapure water. The different volumes of prepared Au solution (0.5, 1.5, 3, and 6 mL) were dropped into aforementioned suspension and then stirred at 40 ℃ for another 12 h. The ultimate materials were obtained through washing, filtration, and drying. The Au modified BGD-1 with 0.5, 1.5, 3, and 6 mL of Au solution were nominated as BGDA-0.5, BGDA-1.5, BGDA-3, and BGD-6, respectively. Meanwhile, the Au modified pristine BMO was prepared using the similar process but replacing BGD-1 with BMO, and it was nominated BA-3 (modified with 3 mL of Au solution).

The composition and crystalline structure of all obtained materials were determined via X-ray diffraction (XRD) using XRD-6100 equipped with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm), and the scanning rate was 8° min−1 in the 2θ range between 10° and 80° (XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Japan). The elements and their valence states were acquired via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with Al-Kα X-ray (hν = 1486.6 eV) radiation (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, United States of America). The functional group of the as-obtained photocatalysts was collected via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Nicolet 5700 FT-IR Spectrometer, USA Thero Electron Corporation, United States of America). The micromorphologies and crystal lattice parameters were obtained through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN TMP, FEI Company, United States of America). The thicknesses of catalysts were estimated by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Dimension Icon, Bruker, Germany). The generated ROS were detected via electron spin resonance (ESR) utilizing 5, 5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a probe (JES FA200, JEOL, Japan).

The photo-electrochemical performances of catalysts were tested on an electrochemical workstation using Ag/AgCl electrode system. The material was loaded on FTO conductive glass and used as working electrode, and Pt sheet and Ag/AgCl electrode were separately applied as counter electrode and reference electrode. In the test, the electrode was immerged in electrolyte solution. Transient photocurrent (TPC) was tested by irradiating working electrode with simulated solar light using 300 W xenon lamp (>420 nm) as light source. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky were all recorded on this electrode system to investigate the physical and chemical property of pristine BMO and its hybrid materials.

The catalytic performances of the various photocatalysts were tested via the photodegradation of OTC under different lighting conditions (>420 nm and > 700 nm), and the light sources are provided by a 300 W xenon lamp with 420 nm and 700 nm cutoff filter (Scheme S1). In general, 10 mg of material was first added 100 mL of OTC (10 mg L-1, it is an OTC water solution and prepared by ultrapure water) and then underwent ultrasound for 10 min to form highly dispersed mixture. The mixture was magnetically stirred for 1 h under dark condition to obtain the adsorption/desorption equilibrium, and then exposed to xenon lamp to conduct degradation experiment. In photocatalytic experiment, 3 mL of sample was extracted out from catalytic system at certain time interval (10 min) and filtrated with 0.45 μm organic microporous membrane to dislodge material from solution. The concentrations of OTC were determined on UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 353 nm.

The crystalline structure of all fabricated material is identified via XRD. The 2θ values at 28.24°, 32.56°, 46.78°, and 55.58° for pure BMO are well matched with (131), (060), (062), and (133) reflections of the standard orthorhombic phase of BMO (Fig. S1a) [25,26]. However, the diffraction peaks ascribed to N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles cannot be detected after incorporation, which is probably owing to the low content and high dispersion of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles (Fig. S1b-c) [21]. Meanwhile, FT-IR result also shows that no related characteristic peaks of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles are discovered in BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3, and only some peaks ascribed to BMO can be found, which further indicates the low content and high dispersion (Fig. S1d and Fig. S2). The contents of N, S-GQDs and Au in BGDA-3 are determined to be 0.37% and 0.18%, respectively. Significantly, the loading amount of Au in this study is much lower than that of previous studies, which can greatly reduce cost (Table S1).

The morphology of BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3 is surveyed by TEM and the result shows that pristine BMO displays ultrathin 2D nanosheet (Fig. 1 a). It is not difficult to observe that many dark spots are existed in BGD-1, which are attributed to N, S-GQDs (Fig. 1b). Meanwhile, some nanoparticles with diameter of 10–15 nm can be easily observed in BA-3 picture, which are ascribed to Au nanoparticles (Fig. 1c). As expected, both dark spot and nanoparticles are all found in BGDA-3, indicating that N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles have been successfully co-modified on ultrathin BMO nanosheet (Fig. 1d). In HRTEM of BGDA-3, two obvious lattice distances of 0.320 and 0.235 nm are detected, which are well-matched with (131) and (111) atomic plane of BMO and Au nanoparticles (Fig. 1e) [27,28]. The thickness of ultrathin BMO nanosheet is measured by AFM and the heights of 0.85 (0.93) is put down to monolayer nanosheet, and 1.75, and 2.35 nm are associated with double layers and triple layers, originating from the overlap of BMO nanosheet because of its high surface energy (Fig. 1f-g). The result reveals that the prepared BMO nanosheet is single atomic layer thickness. In addition, the EDS elemental mapping exhibits that Bi, Mo, O, C, N, S, and Au coexist in BGDA-3 with a great dispersion (Fig. 1h-o), which further confirms the successful introduction of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles on BMO nanosheet.

Meanwhile, the chemical composition of all prepared photocatalysts and chemical environment of various elements were determined via XPS. As shown in Fig. S3, Bi, Mo, and O can be easily found in BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3, while C, N, S, and Au can hardly observe as a result of the low loading of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticle. The high-resolution Bi 4f spectra of BMO show that two binding energies located at about 158.80 and 164.15 eV are well-matched with Bi 4f3/2 and Bi 4f5/2, which indicates its positive trivalent in these photocatalysts (Fig. 2 a) [29]. In high-resolution Mo 3d spectra, the binding energies at 232.10 and 235.25 eV correspond to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2, revealing its positive hexavalent form (Fig. 2b) [30]. The binding energies at 529.80 (529.90) and 531.20 eV (531.30 eV) of O 1 s in BMO (BA-3) are ascribed to lattice oxygen and the adsorption of H2O and –OH (Fig. S4a and Fig. S4c) [31]. In addition, two new peaks at 531.00 (531.60) and 532.10 eV (532.70 eV) are appeared in O 1 s spectra of BGD-1 (BGDA-3), which are in conformity with C = O and C-OH/C-O-C groups, respectively (Fig. S4b and Fig. S4d) [32]. Three main binding energies at 284.65, 285.50, and 286.50 eV of C 1 s are well-matched with the sp2 C in C–C, sp3 C in C-N, and C = O, respectively (Fig. 2c) [33,34]. It’s worth noting that sp2 C is major component in C 1 s spectra, indicating the successful preparation of N, S-GQDs. This is because graphene is made up by sp2- hybridized carbon atoms [35]. N 1 s spectra exhibit predominant positions at 398.40 and 401.10 eV, which are associated with pyrrolic N (C-N-C) and graphitic N or N–H bands, respectively (Fig. 2d) [36]. In addition, two peaks suited at 159.25 and 164.55 eV in S 2p spectra are obviously seen, which is separately related to S 2p3/2 of thiophene and S = O (Fig. 2e) [37]. Meanwhile, Au 4f spectra are differentiated into two peaks at 83.35 and 87.15 eV, belonging to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 of Au in zero oxidation state (Fig. 2f) [38]. As expected, the binding energies of elements in BMO have a slight shift compared to BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3 after the introduction of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles, indicating the intimate contact between BMO, N, S-GQDs, and Au nanoparticles. Since the binding energies in XPS have an inverse correlation with surface electron densities of catalysts, the changes in binding energies reveal the changes in surface electron densities, which further confirms the successful preparation of BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3 [39].

The optical properties of BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3 have been explored via UV–vis DRS technology. The absorption edge of pristine BMO approximately situates at 480 nm, and BGD-1 has a slight red-shift after the introduction of N, S-GQDs (Fig. 3 a). In addition, the absorption edges constantly redshift after continuously adding N, S-GQDs into BMO, and the light intensities also have an enhancement (Fig. S5a). What is interesting is that the light intensities of BA-3 and BGDA-3 have strengthened with the introduction of Au nanoparticles (Fig. 3a and Fig. S5b), and the absorption edge almost red-shift to 800 nm. More importantly, the light intensity in the region of 500–600 nm has a great increment, which is the typical SPR absorption peak of Au nanoparticle [40]. It’s not hard to see that the absorption intensity of BGDA-3 gets a larger increment after the introduction of Au nanoparticles into BGD-1, while only a small increment is observed after the introduction of Au nanoparticles into BMO. This is because that N, S-GQDs absorb and convert a part of NIR light into UV or visible light, further indicating the existence of synergistic effect between Au nanoparticles and N, S-GQDs. All of these evidences testify that the introduction of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles not only broadens the optical absorption range, but also heightens light absorption intensity. The band gaps of various catalysts are determined based on classic formula [41,42], and the band gaps of BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3 are 2.71, 2.65, 2.70, and 2.64 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b and Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. 3c, BMO displays positive slopes in Mott-Schottky plots (500 and 1000 Hz), which indicates that BMO is a n-type semiconductor [43]. The flat band potential of BMO is determined to be −1.05 eV according to the intercept of Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 3c). Normally, the CB potential of n-type catalyst is identical with its flat band potential. In addition, Ag/AgCl potentials are usually transformed to normal hydrogen potential [44]. Consequently, the CB potential of BMO is −0.83 eV and its VB potential is computed to be + 1.88 eV. Besides, XPS-VB technique is also used to determine the CB and VB potential of BMO, and its CB and VB are −0.96 and + 1.75 eV (Fig. 3d), which is nearly equal to the result of Mott-Schottky.

As shown in Fig. S7a, BGDA-3 possesses the smallest arc radius of EIS Nyquist curve among BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3, indicating the smallest resistance for charge carrier migration [41]. In addition, the TPC measurement also indicates that BGDA-3 holds the largest transient photocurrent density in comparison with BMO, BGD-1, and BA-3 (Fig. S7b), suggesting the highest photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency [45]. Besides, the surface charge transfer efficiency of BMO, BGD-1, BA-3, and BGDA-3 are reckoned to be 33.74%, 44.36%, 45.71%, and 53.04%, respectively (Fig. S7c-f). This result also indicates that BGDA-3 holds larger surface charge transfer efficiency after the co-modification of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles.

In this study, OTC is chosen as model pollutant to assess catalytic properties of all prepared catalysts, this is because OTC has been widely used inpoultry and animal farms around the world, while it cannot be utilized by living beings very well, and about ninety percent of OTC are discharged into the environment in the form of their parent or metabolites [46,47]. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4c, OTC is hardly degraded without adding photocatalysts under visible or NIR light illumination, revealing the critical role of photocatalyst in catalytic process. The photocatalytic performance of pristine BMO for OTC degradation is 40.22% under visible light irradiation. The catalytic activity of BMO has a promotion after loading N, S-GQDs, and the optimal BGD-1 (the additive volume is 1 mL) possesses a higher photodegradation efficiency of 54.39% in comparison with pristine BMO (Fig. S8a). Amazingly, the photocatalytic activity of BMO has a great improvement after the co-modification of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles, approximately 81.20% of OTC is degraded within 60 min under the same condition (Fig. 4c and Fig. S8c). In addition, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency can reach to 92.3% after prolonging reaction time to 120 min (Fig. S9). The photocatalytic activity of BGDA-3 is comparable to or even higher than that of most developed materials (Table S2). Meanwhile, the apparent rate constants (kapp) of pristine BMO, BGD-1, and BA-3 for OTC photodegradation are 0.0086, 0.0131, and 0.0148 min−1, while BGDA-3 possesses the highest Kapp of 0.0279 min−1, which is approximate 3.24-fold, 2.13-fold, and 1.88-fold larger than that of BMO, BGD-1, and BA-3, respectively (Fig. 4b, Fig. S8b, and Fig. S8d). The photocatalytic performance of BGDA-3 is further estimated under NIR light illumination owing to the upconversion property of N, S-GQDs and the SPR effect of Au nanoparticle. As exhibited in Fig. 4c, only 7.33%, 13.04%, and 9.21% of OTC can be degraded by BMO, BGD-1, and BA-3 within 6 h. However, BGDA-3 holds a much larger degradation efficiency of 24.47% for OTC in comparison with above-mentioned photocatalysts. This result indicates that the great promotion originates from the synergistic effect (1 + 1 > 2) between N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles rather than additive effect (1 + 1 = 2). This is because that N, S-GQDs harvest and convert NIR light into UV and visible light, and then the SPR effect of Au nanoparticles strengthens the light intensity, and these light sources are eventually captured by BMO. In addition, BGDA-3 also possesses the largest Kapp of 0.0468 h−1 for OTC degradation, which is 3.69-fold, 2.00-fold, and 2.18-fold larger than that of BMO, BGD-1, and BA-3 (Fig. 4d). The aforementioned result confirms that the introduction of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles makes the photocatalytic performance of pristine BMO have an enormous boost and the enormous boost maybe arise from the accelerated charge carrier transfer, the broadened photoresponse range, and the strengthened light intensity.

The practicality is extremely valuable parameter of materials that is intended for practical water treatment. In this study, five actual water matrices of tap water, lake water, river water, industrial wastewater, and medical wastewater are chosen to estimate the practicability of BGDA-3. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, no palpable reduction of photocatalytic performance can be observed when BGDA-3 is applied to the treatment of TW, LW, and RW. Only a slight reduction is found when IW and MW are treated by BGDA-3, which is maybe attributed the coexistence of other organics in these two wastewaters. Nevertheless, the catalytic activity of BGDA-3 for the treatment of actual wastewaters is still acceptable. Moreover, the re-utilizing ability of BGDA-3 is also examined via continuous cycle testing. The result shows that the photocatalytic performance of BGDA-3 for OTC degradation still remains a relatively high value after five successive operations, suggesting its high photostability and strong durability (Fig. 5b and Fig. S10). In order to further measure its stability, the XRD and XPS of BGDA-3 after catalytic reaction are provided, and no obvious difference could be found, confirming its high stability (Fig. S11-13). Based on aforementioned analysis, N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles co-modified ultrathin BMO could be a potential catalyst for environment purification.

The ROS generated in photocatalytic process are detected to explore the catalytic mechanism of BGDA-3 system. In this study, 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine 1-oxyl free radical (TEMPOL), tert-Butanol (TB), and sodium oxalate (SDO) are separately employed as trapping agents of •O2
– , HO•, and h+. The result displays that the catalytic activity of BGDA-3 for OTC photodegradation has a great suppression when TEMPOL and SDO are added into catalytic process, only 40.16% and 44.52% of OTC are degraded within 60 min (Fig. 6 a-b). This result indicates that •O2
– and h+ make great contribution to catalytic reaction. In addition, the addition of TB also poses a negative effect on OTC degradation, suggesting that HO• also takes part in catalytic reaction. Meanwhile, the ESR signal of DMPO-•O2
– with the intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 and DMPO-HO• with the intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 are observed in BGDA-3 under light irradiation, which further confirms the generation of HO• and •O2
– ( Fig. 6c-d). Both capture experiment and ESR characterization prove that •O2
– , HO•, and h+ are chief active species for OTC photodegradation in BGDA-3 system. Significantly, HO• cannot be generated by h+ on the VB of BMO because of the more negative VB position (+1.88 eV<+2.40 eV) based on the energy band analysis. Hence, the generated HO• is originated from •O2
– conversion through molecular oxygen activation.

Meanwhile, the throughput of •O2 – is quantitatively determined through the nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) transformation technique [48]. NBT can make a reaction with •O2 – with a molar ratio of 1:4, and thus resulting in the reduction of absorbance. The throughput of •O2 – is determined by measuring the changes in NBT concentration. The absorbance of NBT has a slight reduction in BMO system, while a larger reduction is observed in BGDA-3 system, indicating that more •O2 – reacts with NBT (Fig. 7 a-b). As shown in Fig. 7c, approximate 5.19% and 22.40% of NBT have made a reaction with •O2 – over BMO and BGDA-3. Moreover, the Kapp for NBT degradation by BGDA-3 is 4.76-fold larger than that of BMO (Fig. S14). Meanwhile, the throughput of •O2 – in BGDA-3 system is 273.93 μmol L-1 g−1, which is much larger than that of BMO (63.43 μmol L-1 g−1) (Fig. 7d). All of these reveal that the molecular oxygen activation ability of BGDA-3 is superior to BMO, further proving that the co-modification of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles is beneficial for molecular oxygen activation.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism is put forward on the grounds of above-mentioned result and analysis (Scheme 2 ). BMO is excited because of its narrow band gap under visible light illumination. The photogenerated electron first flows to the CB of BMO and then transfers to N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles owing to their electron collection effect, thereby the charge carrier separation and transfer efficiency is enhanced. In addition, Au nanoparticles can also generate “hot electron” because of SPR effect, which can inject into CB of BMO by conquering Schottky barrier [49–51]. Meanwhile, the up-conversion photoluminescence property of N, S-GQDs makes it harvest long-wavelength NIR light and convert it into short-wavelength UV or visible light, thereby the long-wavelength light can be utilized in this system and the photoresponse is broadened. In addition, the light nanoantenna property and SPR effect of Au nanoparticles enhance the light intensity. The photogenerated electron on N, S-GQDs or Au nanoparticles and hot electron on CB of BMO activates molecular oxygen to produce •O2 –. Unfortunately, the more negative VB potential of BMO cannot make h+ react with H2O to produce HO•. However, HO• is indeed detected in photocatalytic process according to trapping experiment and ESR characterization. Therefore, the formed HO• stems from •O2 – conversion [52]. In other words, the all generated ROS are originated from molecular oxygen activation and the introduction of N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles is beneficial for enhancing molecular oxygen activation. All produced ROS and photoexcited hole participate in photocatalytic reaction (pollutant degradation).

The photodegradation intermediate products of OTC over BGDA-3 are tested by LC-MS/MS (Table S3) and the possible degradation pathway is exhibited in Scheme 3 . Generally, three possible degradation pathways are observed during the photocatalytic process. In first degradation pathway, the parent OTC is first underwent demethylation by sequential loss of two methyl groups to generate N1 and N2 with m/z of 447 and 433 [53]. Then the generated product N2 draws off a water molecule to produce N3. As for second pathway, the hydroxylation reaction is first occurred on original OTC molecule through the attack of hydroxyl radicals to generate N4 (m/z 475). Then the intermediate N5 is produced through demethylation and deamidation process. In addition, the N5 is turned into N6 with the loss of one amino group [54]. In the last pathway, the parent OTC transforms to N7 through a series of processes, including N-dmethylation, decarbonylation, dehydroxylation, and deamidation [55]. After that, N8 is formed by suffering from demethylation, decarbonylation, and dehydroxylation processes. Subsequently, an amino group and a water molecule are dropped off, resulting in the formation of N9. Ultimately, all intermediates are entirely mineralized into H2O, CO2, and NO3 – as the reaction continues.

In this study, the atomic ultrathin BMO nanosheet is successfully co-modified with N, S-GQDs and Au nanoparticles through facile hydrothermal method and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane coupling method. The obtained hybrid material possesses three superiorities that favor photocatalytic reaction. First of all, the electron collection effect of N, S-GQDs and electron sink of Au nanoparticles accelerate charge carrier separation and transfer. Next in importance, the up-conversion photoluminescence property of N, S-GQDs harvests long-wavenumber light to convert them into short-wavelength light and then absorbed by BMO, thereby broadening the photoresponse range. At length, the light nanoantenna and SPR effect of Au nanoparticles enhance the light intensity. The prepared composite displays excellent catalytic performance in molecular oxygen activation for pollutant degradation in virtue of above-mentioned superiorities, and approximately 81.20% of OTC can be degraded within 60 min, which is much better than that of pristine BMO. In addition, this novel material also possesses NIR-driven photocatalytic activity, about 24.47% of OTC could be degraded under NIR light irradiation. More importantly, this material also shows outstanding catalytic performance for actual wastewater treatment. This work not only develops a strategy to rationally fabricate highly active photocatalyst, but also provides an in-depth comprehension of molecular oxygen activation.
